idioms.definition: Someone or something that is very pleasant or welcome to see, especially after a period of absence or difficulty
examples: After three weeks away on business, her family was a sight for sore eyes.
The sun finally came out after a week of rain – what a sight for sore eyes!
You're a sight for sore eyes – I've been hoping you'd come by all afternoon.
idioms.usageTip: Always a compliment. Used to express that seeing someone or something brings genuine relief or pleasure. Common when greeting someone after a long absence.

idioms.definition: To suddenly become very angry and lose control of your temper
examples: He flew off the handle when he found out the meeting had been cancelled without notice.
She tends to fly off the handle under stress, which makes her difficult to work with.
Don't fly off the handle – let's hear their side of the story first.
idioms.usageTip: Describes a sudden, uncontrolled burst of anger. Often used as a warning or criticism. The image is of a loose axe head flying off its handle unpredictably.

idioms.definition: To waste time feeling upset or complaining about something that has already happened and cannot be changed
examples: Yes, the deadline was missed, but there's no point crying over spilt milk – let's focus on fixing it.
He spent weeks crying over spilt milk after losing the contract.
I know it's disappointing, but don't cry over spilt milk – you'll get another chance.
idioms.usageTip: Usually used as advice to stop worrying about the past. Often introduced with 'no point' or 'there's no use'. Implies resilience and moving forward.

idioms.definition: Experiencing a period of bad luck or misfortune; going through a difficult time
examples: He'd been down on his luck for months after losing his job.
The charity helps people who are down on their luck get back on their feet.
She was down on her luck, but she refused to give up on her dreams.
idioms.usageTip: Suggests a temporary period of misfortune rather than permanent failure. Often used with empathy. Not to be confused with being permanently unsuccessful.

idioms.definition: To feel very annoyed or frustrated with yourself because you made a mistake or missed an opportunity
examples: I could kick myself for forgetting to attach the file to the email.
She'll kick herself when she finds out the tickets were half price yesterday.
Don't kick yourself – anyone could have made the same mistake.
idioms.usageTip: Used to describe self-directed frustration. Common with 'could' and 'will'. Often said as a reassurance: 'Don't kick yourself.'

idioms.definition: To release pent-up stress, energy, or strong emotions through activity, exercise, or talking
examples: After a stressful day, she goes for a run to let off steam.
The children need time outside at lunch to let off steam.
He called a friend to let off steam about his terrible day at work.
idioms.usageTip: Can refer to physical activity (exercise) or verbal expression (venting to someone). Implies a healthy or necessary release of tension. Similar to 'vent'.

fillInExercises.sentence: After six months working abroad, seeing my old friends again was ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'A sight for sore eyes' describes how pleasant or welcome it is to see someone or something after a long time.

fillInExercises.sentence: He completely ___ when he realised his presentation slides had been deleted.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Fly off the handle' means to suddenly and uncontrollably lose your temper.

fillInExercises.sentence: The project failed, but there's no point ___. We need to learn and move on.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Cry over spilt milk' means to waste energy being upset about something that has already happened and cannot be undone.

fillInExercises.sentence: He'd been ___ since the business closed, struggling to make ends meet.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Down on your luck' describes a period of bad luck or hardship.

fillInExercises.sentence: I could really ___ for turning down that job offer – it was such a great opportunity.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Kick yourself' means to feel very frustrated with yourself for making a mistake or missing an opportunity.

fillInExercises.sentence: He goes to the gym after difficult meetings to ___ and clear his head.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Let off steam' means to release stress or strong emotions, often through physical activity or by talking.

lines.speaker: Jamie
lines.text: You're [a sight for sore eyes|a sight for sore eyes]! I've had the worst week imaginable.

lines.speaker: Rosa
lines.text: Me too. I nearly [flew off the handle|fly off the handle] in a meeting yesterday when my manager blamed me for someone else's mistake.

lines.speaker: Jamie
lines.text: That's awful. Did you say anything?

lines.speaker: Rosa
lines.text: I held it together. I went straight to the gym afterwards to [let off steam|let off steam]. Helped a bit.

lines.speaker: Jamie
lines.text: Good call. I keep [kicking myself|kick yourself] over a report I filed with the wrong figures. My manager noticed straightaway.

lines.speaker: Rosa
lines.text: Don't [kick yourself|kick yourself] too hard – everyone makes errors under pressure. No point [crying over spilt milk|crying over spilt milk] now.

lines.speaker: Jamie
lines.text: I know. It's just been one thing after another. I've been feeling a bit [down on my luck|down on your luck] lately.

lines.speaker: Rosa
lines.text: That won't last. Let's have dinner and forget about work for one evening.

lines.speaker: Leon
lines.text: You look exhausted. How are things going at the new job?

lines.speaker: Mia
lines.text: Honestly? Hard. I've been [down on my luck|down on your luck] since I started – nothing seems to go right.

lines.speaker: Leon
lines.text: I'm sorry to hear that. Do you want to [let off steam|let off steam]? I'm happy to listen.

lines.speaker: Mia
lines.text: Yes, please. My supervisor [flew off the handle|fly off the handle] at me in front of the whole team last week. It was humiliating.

lines.speaker: Leon
lines.text: That's completely unprofessional. You should speak to HR.

lines.speaker: Mia
lines.text: I know. I could [kick myself|kick yourself] for not responding more calmly – I just froze.

lines.speaker: Leon
lines.text: Don't [cry over spilt milk|cry over spilt milk]. Focus on what you can do differently next time.

lines.speaker: Mia
lines.text: You're right. And honestly, just seeing you today – you were [a sight for sore eyes|a sight for sore eyes]. I really needed a friend.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: someone or something very pleasant or welcome to see
to waste time worrying about past mistakes that cannot be changed
experiencing a period of bad luck or difficulty
to feel very annoyed with yourself for a mistake or missed opportunity
to release stress or strong emotions through activity or talking

quizExercises.sentence: Try not to ___ about the missed flight – focus on booking the next one.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Cry over spilt milk' means to waste energy being upset about something that has already happened and can't be changed.

quizExercises.sentence: She tends to ___ when things go wrong, so I try to stay calm around her.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Fly off the handle' describes a person who quickly and uncontrollably loses their temper.

quizExercises.question: Which scenario shows someone 'letting off steam'?
options: A manager calmly discussing targets in a quarterly review
An employee venting to a friend about a frustrating day at work
A student quietly reading before an exam
quizExercises.explanation: 'Let off steam' means releasing pent-up stress or emotion, like venting to a friend about a frustrating day.

quizExercises.question: Which person would best be described as 'down on their luck'?
options: A successful entrepreneur celebrating record profits
Someone going through a difficult patch after losing their job and facing unexpected bills
A student who forgot to revise for one small test
quizExercises.explanation: 'Down on your luck' describes someone experiencing a period of misfortune and hardship, not just a minor setback.
